coredata.dc:title: Dual mode detection and sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline with tailor-made N-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: R Thara, Chinnu
Korah, Binila K.
Mathew, Sneha
John, Bony K.
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have emerged as one of the most promising materials with various potential applications derived from their unique photophysical and chemical properties. The present work investigates the electrochemical and photochemical properties of one-pot synthesized carbon dots for environmental sustainability. Facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis of urea and glucose yielded nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-doped carbon dots) with blue fluorescence and a quantum yield of 14.9%. As synthesized N- doped carbon dot had intense fluorescence, stability, water solubility, and biocompatibility. In the sensing studies, N-doped carbon dots appeared as a dual sensor for drug tetracycline with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. Beyond sense, the carbon dots have the potential to act as a photocatalyst for the degradation of tetracycline. Further, N-doped carbon dot could bring exhaustive degradation of tetracycline (>95%) within 10 min in the absence of any additives. This is the first time report on the utilization of raw non-metal doped carbon dots as a photocatalyst for the degradation of tetracycline.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tetracycline
Fluorescent sensing
Electrochemical sensing
Photocatalyst
Photodegradation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of tetracycline in real water samples
Sunlight driven photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and optical properties of N-doped carbon dot: (a) HRTEM images showing low dispersed quasi-spherical carbon dots, (b) lattice resolved images, (c) combination of UV–vis absorption, excitation-emission spectra, and photograph of N-doped carbon dot in short UV, and (d) PL spectra at various excitation wavelengths.

Structure and chemical composition of N-doped carbon dot; (a) XRD pattern, (b) FT-IR spectrum, (c) Raman spectrum of N-doped carbon dot, and (d) High resolution XPS spectra C1s. (e) O1s. (f) N1s spectra.

Sensing of TC with N-doped carbon dot: (a) fluorescent sensing with different drug molecules and quenching of fluorescence with TC, (b) electrochemical sensing TC showing higher current effects, (c) plot of TC concentration Vs. I0/I of N-doped carbon dot during fluorescence, (d) plot of TC concentration vs. current from DPV method, (e) fluorescent selectivity, and (f) electrochemical selectivity.
Mechanism of fluorescent TC sensing: (a) Absorption spectrum of TC and excitation spectrum of N-doped carbon dot. (b) Lifetime of N-doped carbon dot in presence and absence of TC.
Degradation of TC: (a) plot of (C/C0) for TC degradation, and (b) -ln (C/C0) vs. time.
Analysis of TC degradation: (a) UV–vis spectra, and (b) HPLC chromatogram of TC.
Antibacterial study: (a) Standard TC, (b) N-doped carbon dot catalyst, and (c) TC solution after 10 min sunlight irradiation.
Mechanistic analysis of TC degradation: (a) scavenger study, (b) Tauc plot of N-doped carbon dot, (c) impedance spectroscopy, and (d) amperometric analysis.

Photocatalytic activity of N-doped carbon dot: percentage degradation of amoxicillin trihydrate, xylenol orange, levofloxacin, and doxycycline. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic representation of the mechanism of photodegradation of TC.
Analysis of real water spiked with known concentrations of TC by fluorescence and electrochemical methods.

head.ce:title: Dual mode detection and sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline with tailor-made N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have emerged as one of the most promising materials with various potential applications derived from their unique photophysical and chemical properties. The present work investigates the electrochemical and photochemical properties of one-pot synthesized carbon dots for environmental sustainability. Facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis of urea and glucose yielded nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-doped carbon dots) with blue fluorescence and a quantum yield of 14.9%. As synthesized N- doped carbon dot had intense fluorescence, stability, water solubility, and biocompatibility. In the sensing studies, N-doped carbon dots appeared as a dual sensor for drug tetracycline with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. Beyond sense, the carbon dots have the potential to act as a photocatalyst for the degradation of tetracycline. Further, N-doped carbon dot could bring exhaustive degradation of tetracycline (>95%) within 10 min in the absence of any additives. This is the first time report on the utilization of raw non-metal doped carbon dots as a photocatalyst for the degradation of tetracycline.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The enormous advancements made by humanity in technology, domestic life, and industry come at the expense of the environment. All life forms are immediately impacted by aquatic pollution, so it is particularly harmful (Akbar et al., 2021). Tons of residential and industrial contaminants enter marine systems every year. The significant contaminants include residues from skincare and pharmaceutical items, which must be immediately removed from the water. Antibiotic contamination is one of the many ways pharmaceuticals can cause contamination (E Khan et al., 2022; Adhikari et al., 2019). Tetracyclines (TCs) are a type of antibiotic used to treat infections in humans, animals, and aquaculture. Because they are excreted and improperly disposed of, TCs are frequently detected in environmental matrices. The presence of antibiotics in such unexpected places in considerable amounts resulted in the evolution of microbial pathogens with antibiotic resistance (Zhang et al., 2018). Therefore, it is crucial to identify and remove these environmental contaminants. Traditional detection methods typically require sample pretreatment, making measurements that are time-consuming or non-specific. In this situation, a novel method that uses less complex instrumentation is necessary for quickly identifying these pollutants. Photochemical and electrochemical methods are a good substitution for routine methods. It also has the advantages of high sensitivity, ease of sample preparation, low cost, reproducibility, and the lack of organic solvents (Daghrir and Drogui, 2013; Li et al., 2011).

Eliminating pollutants from the ecosystem is equally important to the analytical detection of the contaminants. Compared to numerous methods, a newly developed method has been extensively used for the active destruction of multiple organic and inorganic pollutants by visible light dynamic photochemical process (Chen et al., 2018). Photocatalysis is the term used to describe the energy conversion of materials that utilizes the synergistic interaction of catalysts with light. A new kind of “green” technology called photocatalysis seeks to speed up reactions to degrade pollutants and produce optimal hydrogen energy from sunlight and water. Light-induced charge carriers, electrons, and holes are the first to generate during the photocatalytic reaction. An efficient photocatalyst should be able to separate photogenerated charge carriers with high separation efficiency and should have a wide photoabsorption range (Zhang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017).

CDs are a novel class of materials for photocatalysis that are particularly interesting. Carbon dots exhibit distinctive photophysical and chemical characteristics, including broad-spectrum light absorption, bright photoluminescence (PL), electron mediation, excellent photostability, high aqueous solubility, photosensitization, chemical inertness, effortless functionalization, and low toxicity (Yusuf et al., 2022). Due to their exceptional qualities, CDs have seen a boom in the past two decades in the exploration of their great applications in several fields including photocatalysis, biomedical imaging, sensors, photovoltaic panels, drug delivery, and numerous other technologies. They are advantageous for usage as environmentally benign fluorescence probes for sensing a wide range of target analytes due to their stable and adjustable emission qualities. It can sense trace level of analytes by doping with any metal ion or heteroatom (Kailasa and Koduru, 2022; Kalaiyarasan and Joseph, 2017). The π -π* transition of the sp2 CC double bonds in the inner core gives CDs great ability to absorb light in the UV–vis region. Depending on the synthetic procedure, the outer layer of CDs comprises various functional groups (COOH, OH, NH2, etc.); these groups are responsive to excitation light sources and often absorb in the visible range because of the n-π* transition. When CDs harvest light, electron-hole pairs are formed in them due to the excitation of electrons from the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO). The chemical conversion of the reaction partners is further mediated by these electron-hole pairs (Ding et al., 2021; Tao et al., 2017).

The present finding describes a more straightforward approach for the fabrication of N-doped carbon dots by microwave-assisted synthesis. The CDs acted as dual-functional nano-platform for sensing tetracycline antibiotics through fluorescent sensing and electrochemical sensing. And also as a potential photocatalyst in its pure form for the sunlight-driven degradation of tetracycline. The synthesized material was characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, FT-IR, XRD, HRTEM, etc. In the sensing section, pH dependence, lower detection limit, selectivity, sensitivity, etc., were studied in detail for fluorescent and electrochemical sensing. The degradation of tetracycline with N-doped carbon dots was also studied with various parameters. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report in literature where bare carbon dots were utilized for the photodegradation of antibiotics. And the carbon dots that we came up with can simultaneously sense and photodegrade tetracycline quite effectively in a short time.

Urea, D-glucose, Quinine sulfate, sodium phosphate monobasic, sodium phosphate dibasic, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid, metal chlorides namely potassium chloride (KCl), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2) and chromium chloride (CrCl3), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) of analytical grade, were purchased from Merck (India). Drugs like 5-fluorouracil, doxycycline, tetracycline, amoxicillin trihydrate, cloxacillin, and vancomycin were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used without purification.

In a typical synthesis, 2 g of urea and 1 g of D-glucose were uniformly mixed in 20 ml of double distilled water. The clear solution obtained was then subjected to microwave irradiation for 15 min in a domestic microwave oven (Sharp R-219 T (W)), 2450 MHz). During the treatment in the oven, all the water gets evaporated leaving off-white colored precipitate which then gets charred giving the final product N-doped carbon dots. This product was dispersed in distilled water (50 ml), sonicated, filtered, and centrifuged for purification.

For a typical assay, 100 μL of N-doped carbon dot suspension was diluted with 3.0 ml of double-distilled water, followed by the addition of different concentrations of tetracycline. The mixed solutions were kept static for a few minutes and finally recorded the associated fluorescence quenching spectra with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity without antibiotics is represented as F0, and in the presence of antibiotics is denoted as F.

Determination of quantum yield was accomplished based on a previously established methodology comparing integrated PL intensities. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield 54%) was selected as the reference. N-doped carbon dot and reference solutions were prepared at five different concentrations with absorbance below 0.10 at 360 nm. The quantum yield was calculated with the equation:
[1]
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
μ s
μ R
×
(
η s
η R
)
2
where "ΦS" represents the QY of the sample, "ΦR" represents the QY of the reference, "μs and μR" refer to the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance plot of sample and reference, respectively, "ηR and ηs" refractive indices of solutions of sample and reference, respectively.

Electrochemical studies were carried out using a three-electrode cell in a Biologic SP 200 workstation. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the working electrode, where Ag/AgCl electrode was the reference electrode, and the Platinum wire was the counter electrode. The bare GCE was polished with 0.05 μM sized alumina slurry. A mixture made with the sample and Nafion was drop cast on the electrode surface and dried at room temperature. The analysis was done under inert conditions at room temperature. The sensing response of N-doped carbon dots in real water is also checked for both fluorescence and electrochemical sensing methods.

For the photodegradation experiment, about 800 μg/ml of the catalyst was taken in a 100 ml beaker and 50 ml of tetracycline solution (22 mg/l) was added. The mixture was sonicated for 15 min and further stirred for 45 min in the dark reaching adsorption-desorption equilibrium. About 2 ml of the catalyst containing drug solution was taken and labeled as dark. The bulk catalyst-dye mixture was subjected to sunlight irradiation, and 2 ml of the bulk solution was withdrawn at regular intervals. A spectrophotometer was used to measure the change in absorbance of TC solution after irradiation of sunlight. The studies were carried out in the month of September to October from 12 p.m. to 1 p.m. The degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst was measured by using the following equation
[2]
D e g r a d a t i o n
e f f i c i e n c y
( % )
=
(
C 0
−
C t
)
/
C 0
× 100
where C0 is the initial concentration of the TC and Ct is the concentration of the TC after t min of sunlight irradiation.

Morphology and microstructure of prepared carbon dots were analyzed by HRTEM. Images indicate that N-doped carbon dots have quasi-spherical structure and are not very dispersed (Fig. 1 a). Lattice resolved images show that spacing is 0.27 nm which corresponds to the C(100) plane in the graphitic carbon (Fig. 1b) (Arul and Gopalakrishnan Sethuraman, 2019). SAED pattern shows diffused disk patterns that correspond to the amorphous nature of carbon dot and the average particle size determined from the histogram is 3.09 nm (Fig.S1,2. SI).

The N-doped carbon dots emit blue fluorescence in short UV, while it appears brown colored in visible light. Optical properties are further studied using UV–vis. and fluorescence spectroscopy. N-doped carbon dots exhibit two absorptions, one at 217 nm and a broad absorption ranging from 272 to 350 nm, the former corresponds to the π–π* transition of CC (sp2) bonds, and the latter indicate n–π* transition of CO, and N–H groups. Additionally, the excitation-emission spectra also show a broad excitation peak which corroborates the UV absorptions. The maximum intense emission wavelength is observed at 435 nm when excited at 340 nm (Fig. 1c). Excitation wavelength study displayed strong dependence of N-doped carbon dots emission intensity and wavelength towards the excitation. 300–340 nm excitation resulted in hyperchromic shift and increase in excitation wavelength showed bathochromic, and hypochromic shift. Such a PL spectra of N-doped carbon dot is observed owing to the non-uniform particle size and radiative recombination of carbonyl and nitrogen moieties (Fig. 1d) (Qi et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2012).

Quantum yield of N-doped carbon dot is calculated to be 14.9% using relative fluorescence method with quinine sulfate as the reference sample (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4 at 380 nm). Zeta potential is determined as −27.5Mv at pH 7 (Fig S3, SI). Stability of N-doped carbon dots is analyzed in two ways, storage time and continuous UV irradiation for longer duration. Fluorescent intensity of the carbon dots didn't show any noticeable reduction with time (Fig. S4). Similarly the material photostability is fairly good as the fluorescence intensity is maintained to a great extent after 60 min continuous UV irradiation (Fig. S5).

XRD pattern shows a broad peak at 2θ = 22.80 that corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon based materials with some disordered carbon atoms (Fig. 2 a) due to the tiny size of N-doped carbon dot the peak is broadened. In the FT-IR spectrum, broad bands at 3300-3400 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H groups. The absorption bands at 1712, 1610, and 1445 cm−1 are assigned to the acid carbonyl (OC–O), and sp2-hybridized carbon (CC). The bands at 1320 and 1026 cm−1 are ascribed as the nitrogen bonded with carbon (C–N) and hydroxyl-functionalized carbon (C–OH) groups. Band at 807 cm−1 resembles the triazine unit in carbon nitrides occurred due to the nitrogen doping in carbon dots (Fig. 2b). Further the degree of graphitization is studied with Raman spectroscopy. It showed two bands one at 1352.26 cm−1 and 1562.29 cm−1 ascribed to the disordered sp3 and graphitic sp2 carbon (Fig. 2c).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed for the chemical state and constitution determination of the synthesized nitrogen doped carbon dots. The high-resolution C1s spectrum consists of five peaks. Peaks at 289.3eV, 288.3eV, 286.5eV, 285.3 eV, and 284.6 eV corresponds to CC, CO, C–O, N–CN, and C–C (Fig. 2d). O1s peak is resolved into two peaks 531.8eV (CO) and 532.5eV (C–O) (Fig. 2e). N1s peak comprises of one peak corresponding to 399.9eV (C–N). This nitrogen can be either graphitic one or the edge amino group (Fig. 2f) (Atchudan et al., 2018; Mehta et al., 2014).

A total of six drugs samples were analyzed for the sensing study, where no other drug except TC exhibited significant quenching of N-doped carbon dot fluorescence (Fig. 3 a). The PL intensity drastically decreased when 100 μM TC solution was added to ∼30 μg/mL of carbon nitride dot solution, suggesting that carbon nitride fluorescence is sensitive to TC. A pH study was essential to determine the stability of N-doped carbon dot and the pH-dependent quenching efficiency of TC, which provided the inferences like the relative intensity of N-doped carbon dots didn't show a considerable variation to the acid-base environment supporting its stability. But in the presence of TC; maximum fluorescent quenching was observed at pH 3 (Fig.S6). Therefore further studies were carried out at pH 3.

A systematic study of the variation of fluorescence intensity of N-doped carbon dot with an increase in TC concentration was carried out from 0 μM to 1 μM. With a rise in TC concentration, fluorescence intensity moderately diminished. And no peak shifts were observed, which usually happens in some quantum dots due to scattering, photobleaching, etc. When I0/I were plotted against concentration of TC, a linear graph was obtained between 0 μM–1 μM (Fig. 3c) with R2 value as 0.9986. The limit of detection (LOD) for TC is 56 nM calculated with the equation LOD =
3 σ / S
,
σ
is the slope, and
S
is the standard deviation. A comparison of TC fluorescent detection with carbon dots published in other reports with the present method is given in Table 1(SI). Furthermore, the selectivity of N-doped carbon dot for TC is determined in the presence of other drugs at double concentration as that of TC (Fig. 3e) and the quenching due to other drugs was negligible compared to TC.

As far as the mechanism of TC detection is concerned, quenching of carbon dot fluorescence can occur through the inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Martynenko et al., 2017). They are responsible for the energy transfers, resulting in quenching or fluorescence enhancement. The overlap observed in the absorption spectrum of TC and excitation spectrum of N-doped carbon dots further confirmed the possibility of IFE or FRET (Fig. 4 a) in this particular TC sensing. In FRET, there will be an increase and decrease in the lifetime of carbon dots due to the energy transfer between excited state carbon dot and ground state quencher. On the other hand, in IFT lifetime of the carbon dot remains more or less identical (Qian et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018). Lifetime analysis of N-doped carbon dots in the presence and absence of TC measured 7.89ns and 8.0 ns (Fig. 4b), which is almost the same. Therefore, it is clear that the inner filter effect is responsible for the fluorescence quenching.

For the analysis, about 50 μg/mL of the carbon nitride dot solution was dispersed in Nafion solution (0.5%). About 10 μL aliquot from the mother solution was drop casted on the GCE surface and allowed to dry naturally. Such a modified working electrode was used for further sensing experiments of TC. The sensitivity of the N-doped carbon dot modified electrode for the detection of TC was determined with cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments. Fig. 3b clearly describe the enhanced sensing capacity of the developed sensor in the CV studies with the potential between −1.0 and 1.0 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The surface functional groups of N-doped carbon dot bind with TC, resulting in enhanced anodic and cathodic responses compared to the bare and modified electrode. Also, the introduction of N-doped carbon dot in GCE leads to an increase in surface area and high electrical conductivity, which cumulatively results in the sensing of TC (Palisoc et al., 2019). In the pH study maximum anodic peak was obtained at pH 6 (Fig.S7) so further analysis was done at this optimized pH. The limit of detection of TC with the developed sensor was determined with the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. For the analyte, the concentrations varied from 0.1 to 0.6 μM, and the feedback was increased peak current (Fig.S8). Further, a plot of concentration of analyte versus peak current depicted a linear relation from which LOD is calculated as 21 nM (Fig. 3d). Table 2 (SI) LOD obtained for electrochemical TC detection with materials reported in other reports is compared with our modified electrode. Chronoamperometric technique from 0 to 200 s was employed to evaluate the behavior of the modified electrode to possible interferences during the drug detection. TC was selectively detected in the presence of interferents like vancomycin, 5-fluorouracil, amoxicillin trihydrate, doxycycline, and cloxacillin (Fig. 3f).

A change in the current response with the introduction of TC explains the possible mechanism of electrochemical sensing. The overall current response of the N-doped carbon dot/GCE modified electrode increases in the presence of TC, especially the anodic current much higher when compared to other drugs. It can occur due to the oxidation of some functional groups in TC, like the oxidation of the phenolic group to ketone. Similarly, at the optimized pH-6 TC can undergo protonation. These positively charged molecules can thus interact with negatively charged species like COO- and OH- on the surface of the carbon dot. Through this interaction, TC is electrochemically sensed (Raykova et al., 2021; Fernandes-Junior et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of tetracycline in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The reasonable use of synthesized N-doped carbon dots and the N-doped carbon dot fabricated electrode for the detection TC in real water samples was checked with fluorescence spectroscopy, and DPV method. Tap water from Mahatma Gandhi University and Lake water from Alappuzha district, Kerala collected, filtered, and centrifuged to remove contaminants. These water samples at pH 3 (for PL) and pH 6 (for electrochemical) spiked with known concentrations of TC before analysis. Table 1 shows the excellent detection of TC in the spiked real samples with RSD (%) of 0.02–0.40.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sunlight driven photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition to the well-known traditional applications of CD, the present work deals with the more recent usage of N-doped carbon dot for the photodegradation of TC under the influence of natural sunlight. A comparative degradation study in dark, UV light, and sunlight better explains the significance of solar light for the particular reaction. A plot of relative change in TC concentration as a function of time is the continual drop in TC concentration caused by the N-doped carbon dot interaction under the effect of sun light. Fig. 5 a shows the aqueous phase degradation of TC in dark, UV light, and sunlight in the presence of carbon dot. The same figure explicitly indicates the importance of visible solar light in the degradation of TC with N-doped carbon dot with degradation efficiency above 95% within a span of 10 min. As in the dark as well as in UV light almost no degradation was observed. Additionally according to the equation,
[3]
− ln
C / C o = k t
where k and t are the rate constant and time, respectively; the photocatalytic degradation reaction is considered to follow pseudo-first-order kinetics (Fig. 5b).

The degradation of TC with visible solar light was studied with UV–vis. absorbance and HPLC analysis. In the absorption spectra (Fig. 6 a) the peak of TC is slightly shifted under the influence of N-doped carbon dot from 358.26 nm to longer wavelength 373.26 nm. In the spectra with time the peak reduces and after 6 min no significant peak is observed, after 10 min the spectrum is flat except the peak of N-doped carbon dot. HPLC analysis further confirmed the degradation of TC. Standard TC has major peak at retention time 3.60 min and minor peak at 3.33 min. TC major peak drastically decreased within 4 min of irradiation and further reduced with 10 min (Fig. 6b).

Utilizing high resolution mass spectrometry, TC degradation pathway and degradation product were examined (Fig. 7 ). Mass spectra were recorded with the TC samples collected at various time intervals of irradiation. It's observed that tetracycline with m/z = 444.4 is completely degraded to non-toxic products like butanoic acid, CO2, and H2O over a period of 10 min (Fig.S9). Thus, N-doped carbon dots are potential photocatalyst that can bring almost complete degradation of TC over a short span of time with the aid of visible solar light. The disadvantage with this particular catalyst is that it is highly dispersible in water. So the whole reaction is a homogeneous photocatalytic reaction where there is zero percent recovery of catalyst.

In practical application, the presence of catalyst in the water sources after the degradation of pollutants can also pose a threat as it can be non-biocompatible. So the biocompatibility of N-doped carbon dot is analyzed with cytotoxicity studies (Fig.S10) and found to be non-toxic. Further the observations of antibacterial study with TC, N-doped carbon dot, and photo degraded TC sample (obtained after 10min) on gram positive Staphylococcus aureus bacteria supported the degradation of TC as the standard TC solution were very efficient in inhibiting Staphylococcus aureus, were as TC obtained after solar irradiation had no effect on them (Fig. 8 ) zone of inhibition with TC, N-doped carbon dot, and degraded TC is given in Table 3 (SI).

The feasible photoreaction mechanism in TC photodegradation along with the involved phases and paths are described in Fig. 11 . This explains a well-established hypothesis about the application of surface functionalities to surface defects found on the surface of N-doped carbon dot, which were used in this case to capture photoexcited electrons from sunlight. Reactive oxygen radicals are produced as reaction intermediates (stage II) on the surface of N-doped carbon dot as a result of photoexcitation of electrons in the graphitic phase of carbon dot, which slows down electron-hole pair recombination. This makes oxidative valence electrons for the breakdown of TC easier. The adsorbed TC molecules on the N-doped carbon dot surface react with hydroxyl radicals (stage IV) and break down into smaller hydrocarbons (stage V) (Bhati et al., 2018).

The current work briefly describes the option of fabricating low cost, environment friendly, and high quantum yield nitrogen doped carbon dots as versatile nanomaterial with multiple applications like dual sensor (fluorescent and electrochemical) for the selective and sensitive detection of tetracycline as well as an efficient photocatalyst for visible light degradation of TC at the cost of abundantly available sunlight. The fluorescent and electrochemical studies unanimously showed excellent sensing ability of N-doped carbon dot towards TC. Limit of detection of TC by dual mode detection method are 56 nM and 21 nM respectively. This is first time were raw non-metal doped carbon dot is used as a photocatalyst. And N-doped carbon dot showed the potential for the complete degradation of TC to non-toxic end products within 10 min via homogenous photocatalytic reaction. Additionally the reaction occurs with the visible light, which is the major fraction in the solar spectrum. Thus, N-doped carbon dots hold the potential to function as dual mode sensor for TC, as well as photocatalyst for the aqueous phase photodegradation process.

Chinnu R Thara: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing-original draft. Binila K Korah: Data curation and visualization. Sneha Mathew: Data curation and visualization. Bony K John: Data curation and visualization. *Beena Mathew: Supervision, Draft correction. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Chinnu R Thara is grateful to Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) for Senior Research Fellowship, and Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India for instrumentation facilities provided by DST-PURSE Phase II to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India.

ACS Omega
